name: V8 irregexp
summary: V8's tiered regex engine: bytecode interpreter at startup, native tier-up for hot patterns, restricted-linear-fallback path for safe-subset regexes. Full ECMAScript regex semantics including backtracking; the linear path is only available when the pattern doesn't use backreferences or lookbehind.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**irregexp** is V8's regex engine, used by Chrome, Node.js, and any embedded V8. The architecture is tiered: a bytecode interpreter handles initial executions of any pattern; hot patterns tier up to native code; a restricted-linear path is available for ECMAScript-regex subsets that don't require backtracking semantics.

irregexp implements full ECMAScript regex including lookaround, backreferences, named groups, and Unicode-mode features. The full feature set forces backtracking semantics — patterns can produce exponential time on adversarial input — but the tiered design speeds up the common case.

sections.title: Restricted linear fallback
sections.content:
V8 added a restricted-linear path for regexes that don't require backtracking: patterns without backreferences, lookbehind, or recursive subpatterns can be compiled to an NFA-style linear-time matcher. For workloads where such patterns are common (most server-side input validation), the linear path provides RE2-style guarantees within an ECMAScript-compatible API.

The fallback is automatic: V8 checks whether a pattern is in the linear-eligible subset and chooses the linear engine if so; otherwise the standard backtracking engine runs. This is the regex equivalent of V8's tiered-JIT design — common cases pay no penalty for the exotic-features support.
